sentences.text: Freddy Ephraim Diament.
Freddy.
No.
Ephraim is my Hebrew name but I used it as a middle name later in life.
I was born in Gelsenkirchen, in Germany.
It is located in the Ruhr valley, the industrial belt of Germany.
March the 30th, 1924.
I know only what I was told by my mother and my father.
Personally, I did not know them.
They lived in Poland.
Right.
Weiss.
Amalia.
Mm-hmm.
Her father's name was Saul.
Saul.
That I know.
Her mother's name, I don't remember.
Yeah.
They lived in Poland.
Near Krakow.
In [NON-ENGLISH].
There's a Jewish name for it.
[YIDDISH], they called it, in Yiddish.
Not far from Krakow.
Yeah.
What-- what my mother told us about them.
I know that he was a merchant.
And I also knew that he got-- besides he was a-- a Talmudic scholar.
I also know that he spoke German.
And my mother was, every-- every time, she was so proud.
When the Austrians in the First World War entered the city, he wrote the statute in German.
He was educated enough that he could write it down for the occupying forces.
Because they chased away the Russians, the Austrian army.
Oh yeah.
She had--
More or less.
I know one-- the youngest one was Josef, also Weiss.
Then they had a sister, was Hilda Weiss.
And she had an older brother.
His name was [INAUDIBLE] Weiss.
He lived in Berlin before the war.
And then she had the oldest sister, [?
Helen, ?] lived in the United States, in New York.
Oh, she-- wait a minute.
She had another brother.
One was [?
Nathan, ?] and he lived also in Germany.
And then she had a younger brother, [?
Shmuel, ?] who went in 1923 to Israel.
He was a-- a pioneer [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
Those in Israel, for sure, survived.
But those in-- in-- in-- in the occupied territories, none survived.
To the best of my knowledge.
I did some research.
I couldn't find anybody.
As a matter of fact, my name is after my grandfather.
His father's name was Ephraim.
And his mother's name was [INAUDIBLE].
Also in Poland.
They lived in a small town, also not far from Krakow, called Lancut.
No.
Lancut.
As a matter of fact, they've found now the best-maintained synagogue, that was built in the 15th century.
And it's very famous.
It was all restored and became almost like a museum.
Lancut?
L-A-N-C-- with a little-- underneath-- U-T.
Lancut.
Yeah.
As a matter of fact, the-- the city is very known that the famous Graf Potocki had a giant liquor factory in the city.
That's what the city is famous for.
And to the best of my knowledge, it still-- the factory still exists.
He-- he was also-- he had-- I think that he had a grocery store.
Yeah.
He also was-- was a-- a [NON-ENGLISH].
He was very much involve in-- in-- in-- in studying Torah.
And to the best of my knowledge, his wife ran the business more than he did.
He was studying day and night, so to speak.
Yeah.
They had a lot of children.
I think eight or nine.
I remember the-- the names of the brothers.
One was Yaakov.
And he lived in [POLISH], also a city, probably 50 miles north of-- of-- of Lancut.
And the other name was David.
And some of his children lived in Israel.
I knew them.
And he lived in a-- in a city, very well-known city-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- called [NON-ENGLISH].
Also not far from Krakow.
They all lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
Then he had a sister, Bertha, who lived in [NON-ENGLISH] in-- in-- in-- in [NON-ENGLISH], not far from my hometown, in Germany.
Who else is there?
Let me see.
Yeah.
And then he had a-- a-- a sister, Ida.
As a matter of fact, the interesting thing is she was married to a man.
They were very poor.
And they, when the Nazis occupied Poland, fled to Russia.
And they had nine children.
And all survived.
And now--
--They live in-- in Brooklyn.
She all-- they all have great grandchildren.
And they are all Chabad Hasidim.
Lubavitcher Hasidim.
Very active.
Very, very active.
They must be-- the family must be today with children, great-- grandchildren, great-grandchildren, at least a hundred.
It's amazing.
No.
She was a Diament.
Her husband was-- the name was Friedman.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It was the sister.
Yeah.
I told you that--
In Israel, that's all.
Yeah.
And there were plenty.
I know that-- that-- that-- the-- the oldest brother, Yaakov, had 10 children.
My father used to tell me that.
And I couldn't find anybody.
Yeah.
I-- I go to the committee in Poland and-- and-- and-- and even right after the war, I passed by in those towns.
Couldn't find anybody.
Simon.
Shimon in Yiddish.
And my mother's name was-- was Amalia.
My father, interesting enough, until the age of 28, he never worked a day.
He was a [NON-ENGLISH].
He was really, in the true sense of the word, a Talmudic scholar.
He-- he knew whole pages by heart, even then.
And then, when they came to Germany in 1919, and they married, also, right away, in-- in-- they met in Poland, he and my mother.
And then they married in-- in Duisberg.
It's also a city, between Dusseldorf and Gelsenkirchen.
And then he went into business and he opened up a textile store in Germany.
I forgot.
Honestly.
But they met-- they met-- they-- they were introduced.
What you call a Shidduch.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Wait a minute.
I think they were introduced through a brother of my mother.
Now I remember.
Nathan. ?] He introduced him.
I think they were together in-- in-- in the army.
Now I remember that.
Yeah.
The both were in the Austrian Army in the First World War.
They were more or less the same age.
And he introduced-- [?
Nathan ?] introduced his sister to my father.
That's the way it was.
Now I remember it.
And-- and [?
Nathan ?] survived the war, also in Israel-- in Palestine, so to speak.
And he told me a story.
Now I remember that.
Yeah.
Without the parents, just by themselves.
Their parents were-- were-- were dead already.
They have died.
Yeah.
They died at a relatively young age.
In the late 50s they died.
The father and the mother.
I think that-- they died in the First World War.
There must have been an epidemic or something.
Yeah.
Let me say this.
My parents were-- my father, particularly, were-- was a modern Orthodox Jew.
And they were [NON-ENGLISH] Shabbat.
They-- and we were one of the very few families in our town, in Gelsenkirchen, where the business-- the store-- was close on the Shabbat.
Although it was a major business day, they really lived by what they pretended to-- to be.
And my father was very active in the Jewish community.
Some people, they are-- they are very religious.
But when it comes to money, they compromise.
So he lived by what he-- what he-- what he believed in, so to speak.
And I was not the first one.
I was the third child.
We were three boys that were born one after the other, with a year difference.
And I remember very well how proud he was when he went to the shul with the three boys.
Like a [INAUDIBLE] you know?
Big, small, smaller.
And we got a very intensive Jewish education.
As a matter of fact, first we went to cheder.
But then, we had a private teacher, what they called in-- in-- in Yiddish, a melamed, that came to our house.
It was very expensive.
But my father insisted on this.
And we got quite an intensive Jewish education-- at least for Germany.
The-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], so-- the reason--
My oldest brother name is Saul.
And as a result of this, he became a rabbi later, in United States.
My brother is-- the oldest brother is the only one that was not in the Shoah.
He got out of Germany in the last minute, practically, in 1939, and went illegally to Israel, over [NON-ENGLISH].
And he arrive with a ship on the same day as the Second World War broke out, on September the 1st.
And they were seized by-- the ship was seized by the British.
And they just all jump into the sea and dynamited the ship.
So.
The-- the wreck of the ship is still in Tel Aviv harbor [INAUDIBLE].
And he arrived in Israel in his swim trunks.
That's all he had.
He was lucky that he had two uncles in-- in-- in Tel Aviv.
The two younger brothers of my mother.
They took them-- the youngest uncle took him like a-- almost adopted him.
And he lived there with his uncle for-- for-- for two years.
And then he joined a kibbutz.
That's my brother.
Then he came to United States later.
He was with me, together, and my father, in the concentration camps.
Leo.
Leo Yehuda.
Since he also was a member of the [NON-ENGLISH] group, he called himself the Hebrew name, Yehuda.
And he was with me together for almost five years.
And then he has executed.
He was hanged by the Nazis in-- in-- in Auschwitz, because he was an active member of the underground.
And he and two his friends-- they were also hanged-- were actively involve in preparing a mass outbreak.
Later.
Later.
Yeah.
Yeah.
There was my sister.
My sister, Elly.
She went with my mother in-- let me put it this way.
We were arrested in-- in--
OK.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
She-- she-- she was younger.
She was two years, three years younger than me.
She went to school.
And then I had two smaller children.
One is-- was named [?
Sammy, ?] who also went to school.
He was-- before the Holocaust, he was probably 12 years old.
And then I had a very young brother, was only six years old.
He was-- his name was Martin.
So we were six children.
Five boys and one girl.
We have quite a very good family life.
My-- I forgot to mention before, my father also had a younger brother, [INAUDIBLE], who also lived in our town.
I forgot to mention it.
And he died in between now.
And he first had in partnership with my father at the store.
And then he got married, was introduced to my mother-- to-- to his wife.
And then they split up.
And he changed his name, interesting enough, to [?
Diamant, ?] so that people don't get mixed up.
And-- and they both were very active involve in the Jewish-- in the [GERMAN] community.
There were two communities in Germany.
The-- call it indigenous German Jews.
They had their own synagogue.
And the Jews that came from Poland, they called them [GERMAN], eastern Jews.
So we belonged, of course, since my parents came from Poland, we belonged to the [GERMAN] community.
And there was a very active-- and my father was all the time in the committee, in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE], they called this.
And he also-- I remember once in awhile, he gave courses and taught other people the Talmud Gemara.
And-- but I very much remember is, Shabbat was really Shabbat.
Was a day of-- of pray, a day of resting.
And it was almost a tradition in Germany, after the-- the-- the-- the davening, after the prayer, they went to the park.
Each city had a beautiful park.
And you could see only Jews in the park.
Beautiful park with flowers, with-- with-- with-- with a little artificial lake.
And then we went home for dinner.
And-- and-- and after dinner, my father used to give us always a lecture about the midrash.
That was the most interesting thing.
Because the midrash is really-- that tells the story of the Bible, more more-- let's put it this way-- in detail than the-- than the-- the Torah that you read, you know?
And it was most interesting when he gave us-- midrash means, really, explanation.
The [HEBREW] in Hebrew.
That means to more explain the text of the Torah.
And-- and that-- that was so interesting.
And sometimes we said more, more, more.
Although we were-- we-- we are learning all year round in cheder, and then when the-- the teacher came to our house.
But the midrash was very interesting.
Was really a-- quite a-- let's put it this way-- a treat.
That's the word.
Was quite a treat.
My parents were observing Jews.
We were strictly kosher in the house.
We celebrate all the Jewish holidays, the way it should be.
And as I said, we got a very intensive Jewish education.
All the children.
Including my-- my-- my sister.
But not from the-- from the melamed.
Not from the teacher.
She went to a-- to a day school there.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
The whole family.
We were walking, and sitting, and meeting other friends.
It was-- but it was a meeting place, so to speak.
No.
No.
No.
No.
We ate at home.
No.
As I said, my parents wouldn't eat anywhere.
I-- I don't think we had a kosher restaurant in our city.
I don't think so.
Not very often.
We met her in the park, as a matter of fact.
She went on holidays.
But not every Shabbat.
My father went every Shabbat, and even every day, sometimes.
Oh.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, are you kidding me?
We were fighting day and night.
We-- we had-- in our house, it was-- always something was going on.
Because even the little ones, when there are five boys-- we were fanatic soccer players.
And we always pretended we had our own team.
Because there's three, and then we-- we had friends.
And they're constantly-- you know, in-- in-- in-- at that time in Germany, everybody played soccer, even in the street.
And we lived in an area where there was very little traffic.
So-- in-- in a very, very beautiful home we had there.
And then we were playing soccer.
And-- and-- belonged-- we belonged, also, to a Jewish sports club, the Hakoah.
We didn't-- there was-- there were two competing sports club-- Maccabi and Hakoah.
Oh my god.
I don't remember.
Soccer balls.
I mean, did you have more than one?
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no.
I-- I did most of the fighting.
Because my brother, the oldest brother, wanted to be the boss all the time.
And I didn't accept that.
I said, wait a minute.
Wait a minute.
There is no birth right that you have [INAUDIBLE].
And so there were arguments going on.
You know.
But-- but in-- in general, we stuck together, let's put it this way.
Obviously.
He was the peacemaker.
He was the peacemaker.
The second brother was always the peacemaker.
And if something was going on, I remember his-- he was such an altruist, that's the word.
When-- when we did something wrong, and my parent want to know who did it, when something was broken, he volunteered always.
He volunteered.
He was such an idealist.
Yeah.
And all his life, he was an idealist.
That's probably the reason that he got in trouble, and-- and-- and didn't survive.
Yeah.
I-- I-- interesting enough, she complains even now.
I was her boss.
Everybody had a boss.
I commandeered her around.
Because I was the older brother.
The two oldest brothers were very close.
And-- and-- and then-- there are always cliques when you have a lot of children.
And I was close to my sister.
So I-- I helped her.
I went to high school.
She went to high school.
To do gymnasium.
She went to lyceum.
And-- and I remember she had problems with Latin.
I helped her a little bit.
I was-- I was a good student, so-- there was a relationship, very close.
And-- although I disciplined her, so to speak.
Because I was the older brother.
But I stuck up for her and she stuck up for me.
No, no, no.
No, no, no.
I can remember only-- he-- he caught me smoking when I was 12 years old.
That's the only time he hit me.
With a--
With a stick.
With [GERMAN].
I mean, it didn't hurt, really.
So, but what-- so to speak, symbolically.
He wanted to, to make it clear to me that this is not acceptable, period.
No.
No.
No.
She was a very devoted mother.
But she also was very active in the business.
See, my father was not a businessman.
My mother was the-- I was told by my mother when she was 16 years old, she moved to Vienna.
I don't know if you know that, that they say, 80% of the Viennese were Galaciano, practically.
Their parents all came from Galicia and they moved to Vienna.
So he moved to Vienna with a friend and opened up a textile store, for-- for-- for fashion textiles, so to speak.
She liked fashion very-- as a young girl.
And-- and she had the-- the experience.
She was a born businesswoman.
It was in her blood, so to speak.
She was very good at it.
And having six children and being active in the store was very interesting.
And she found the time.
Of course, at that time, we did pretty good.
We had a maid and a governant.
Governess?
Governess.
A governess.
For the children.
Because she-- somebody had to take care of the children.
Especially the little ones, you know.
So she could find the time to be in-- in-- in-- in the business.
She was-- she was loving.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Even my father was not that strict.
I went to-- all children went.
All the three brothers.
The oldest, the second, and me went to gymnasium.
We were at-- at the age already.
And my sister, Elly, went to lyceum.
To the best of my knowledge, she was only 10 years-- she went, 2 or 2 and 1/2 years she went.
Until 1938, where we all were thrown out of school.
We-- we were allowed to go to-- to the-- to high school, which was unusual, because we were considered Polish citizens.
If we would have been Germans, they would have thrown us out earlier.
They-- for some reason, that is not very logical, the Nazis, in order to maintain good relations with foreign countries, allowed Jews to go to public high schools if they were foreign citizens.
It's interesting.
If we would-- the German Jews-- I remember, I had in my class, two boys-- they were German Jews-- in 1936, after the Nuremberg Laws came out, after the Olympics, they were thrown out of school.
They were not allowed, anymore, to school.
And-- so I was Jewish, but I was considered a foreign citizen-- or my parents were foreign citizens.
They allowed us to stay in school until Kristallnacht.
Oh my god, and how.
It was a frightening experience.
That really-- the Kristallnacht-- was, so to speak, I would say, the general rehearsal of the Nazis for the Holocaust.
It really was.

Because until that night, although there were boycotts, there were individual persecution of-- of-- of individuals, there were the Nuremberg Law, that deprived the Jews of making a living, throwing them out from all their professions.
They were not allowed to work in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in universities.
They were totally outcast.
But they still could make a living.
And they-- they were boycotting the stores with signs to don't buy from the Jews.
The Jews are our-- our-- our-- our-- our-- [GERMAN]-- our misfortune, so to speak.
But in Kristallnacht, [? it ?] was a very frightening experience.
And-- and I remember on November the 9th, first of all, we saw the-- the synagogue was inside where we lived.
We saw the synagogue burning.
And we heard the noise and the screaming.
Most of those, they were not SS.
They were SA.
[GERMAN], they called them.
They were not as young.
They were more of the elderly.
[GERMAN].
Yeah.
SR, they call it.
SA in English.
And some of them were literally drunk.
And they went from store to store.
And there were few other Jewish stores, not much further we lived.
In Germany at that time, it was customary that you had a store on the ground floor and you lived upstairs.
We lived in the fourth floor.
It was a nice building.
And I remember we heard the screaming.
And-- and-- and it broke all the-- the-- the-- the big windows.
And-- and by force, they-- they-- they broke the-- the-- the-- the iron door and-- and went-- and started destroying everything and burning everything.
We smelled the-- the fire.
And then, the most frightening thing was, they were knocking at the door.
There was a very strong door to the entrance to the apartments.
And they scream all the time, are there Jews, more Jews, in that house?
What they did in many instances, they beat up people.
They were-- they were drunk.
And the police did not interfere.
The police had strict-- we found out after, two days later-- everybody, every Jew had one friend at the police.
And they mostly informed of what was going on, even among the gestapo, interesting enough.
There were Germans that somehow knew some of the Jewish citizens for years and years, at least informed him, watch it.
There is an action being planned.
Hide, don't be at home, what have you.
Interesting enough, there were actions by the Nazis, by the Gestapo, when you were not at home, you were-- you were lucky.
I give an example.
If you remember-- let me tell you a story about the Kristallnacht.
They were knocking at the door and wanted to get up.
And fortunately enough, on the top floor, there was a very poor German family.
My mother was very philanthropic, so to speak.
Poor people, if they were Jewish, or Germans, she helped him.
From the store, stuff that didn't sell, she'd give to-- [INAUDIBLE] she had six children.
And one of her sons, I think it was the oldest son, became an SS man.
SS man.
And-- and-- and-- and he did not make nasty remarks to us.
Because his mother didn't tolerate it.
And we begged the mother, please, tell your son to go to the window and tell the-- the people downstairs there are no Jews here.
And let him put on his SS uniform.
She didn't want to do that.
And my mother begged her and cried.
Eventually, he did it.
And he went to the window.
And that really saved us from being hurt.
I'll give you an example.
There was a family about two blocks from us.
They had a big store, a very big store, one of the biggest shoe stores.
And they also lived on the second floor in the same house.
They literally took the man and the woman and threw 'em out of the window.
Just like that.
As if they were garbage.
And the man was totally paralyzed after this.
The woman was sick and broke her leg or her arm.
But the husband was paralyzed after this.
That we heard after the event.
And we-- but we were afraid that they will hurt us.
